abstract_ts: Venation patterning in leaves is a major determinant of photosynthesis efficiency because of its dependency on vascular transport of photo-assimilates, water, and minerals. Arabidopsis thaliana grown in microgravity show delayed growth and leaf maturation. Gene expression data from the roots, hypocotyl, and leaves of A. thaliana grown during spaceflight vs. ground control analyzed by Affymetrix microarray are available through NASA's GeneLab (GLDS-7). We analyzed the data for differential expression of genes in leaves resulting from the effects of spaceflight on vascular patterning. Two genes were found by preliminary analysis to be up-regulated during spaceflight that may be related to vascular formation. The genes are responsible for coding an ARGOS (Auxin-Regulated Gene Involved in Organ Size)-like protein (potentially affecting cell elongation in the leaves), and an F-box/kelch-repeat protein (possibly contributing to protoxylem specification). Further analysis that will focus on raw data quality assessment and a moderated t-test may further confirm up-regulation of the two genes and/or identify other gene candidates. Plants defective in these genes will then be assessed for phenotype by the mapping and quantification of leaf vascular patterning by NASA's VESsel GENeration (VESGEN) software to model specific vascular differences of plants grown in spaceflight.

abstract_ts: Images of the alpha-amylase molecule from pig pancreas have been obtained in two projections by the application of spatial filtering techniques to electron micrographs of negatively stained microcrystals of the enzyme. The Fourier filtering was performed on a PPD 11/40 digital computer after microdensitometry on an Optronics P-1000 photoscan system. The set of procedures and programs we employed are described herein. We compared these images with equivalent images obtained by three-dimensional X-ray diffraction analysis using conventional isomorphous replacement at approximately twice the resolution. We find that there is quite good agreement between the two kinds of images, and that a number of gross structural features compare quite well. We conclude that the combination of electron microscopy with digital spatial filtering when applied to protein microcrystals yields very respectable results.

abstract_ts: Objectives: Acute procedural success and safety of catheter-based mitral valve repair using the MitraClip system have been documented previously. We report our mid-term data of mitral valve function and clinical outcome after MitraClip therapy in a selected patient cohort not suitable for surgery. Methods: A total of 104 consecutive patients (age 74±9 years; 62% male) who had undergone MitraClip implantation for symptomatic functional or degenerative mitral regurgitation (MR) grade ≥3+ was followed for a median of 359 (248–404) days. At clinical visits, echocardiography and clinical assessment were performed, and patients were subjected to a structured interview including Minnesota Living with Heart Failure Questionnaire. Results: Successful percutaneous MitraClip implantation was achieved in 96 patients (92%). In these patients, MR grade 1+ or 2+ was present in 83% at 1-year follow-up. Median left ventricular end-diastolic volume (baseline: 221 (174–281)ml vs. follow-up: 183 (150–233)ml; p<0.0001) and end-systolic volume (baseline: 125 (87–193)ml vs. follow-up: 102 (73–166)ml; p=0.0002) were significantly reduced, whilst median forward stroke volume (baseline: 43 (31–53)ml vs. follow-up: 49 (42–59)ml; p<0.0001) was significantly increased. Improvement of NYHA functional status was observed in 80%, with 69% of patients in NYHA I or II. This was further evidenced by increase in 6-minute walk test in 75% of patients, and by improvement in quality-of-life results in 74% of patients. One-year estimates of mortality and rehospitalisation were 22% and 31%, respectively. Six (6%) patients needed surgical valve repair/replacement due to failed interventional repair or recurrent severe MR. Conclusions: Mitral valve repair using the MitralClip system significantly improved clinical and echocardiographic outcome at one year in a subgroup of symptomatic patients with prohibitive surgical risk. However, overall 1-year mortality reflects severity of the patients comorbidities and the natural course of the disease.
